entries.question: 1. Executive summary
entries.answer:
There is one apparatus, faced three ways. The bet is **stored compute**: *compute is the binding constraint on AI, so whoever turns compute into an owned, bankable, verifiable asset captures the value others rent.* The keyword is **stored** — not compute you run, compute you bank — and there are three stores that are the same venture seen from three sides (`STORED-COMPUTE-THESIS.md` §0):

- **Store the input** — energy, the credit-subsidized cost-base of compute (MWh / tax-credit dollars).
- **Store the capacity** — compute capability turned into a tradable, pre-sold instrument ($/offtake, token fractions).
- **Store the output** — the *result* of compute, sealed and validated into reusable truth tokens.

All three sit on the **same ~15 Las Vegas affordable-housing buildings**. The honest reframe — and the part that survives diligence — is that the three stores are **not welded by electrons, they are welded by one machine**: the same sites, federal credits, entity stack, tokenization, and provenance ledger that bank stored energy also bank stored capacity and stored computation. **The binding is ownership + financing + provenance, not physics** (`STORED-COMPUTE-THESIS.md` §1). We do not claim the batteries run the models; we claim one apparatus turns energy, capacity, and computation each into an ownable, financeable, verifiable asset. That version is the moat.

entries.question: 1. Executive summary
entries.answer:
The near-term, hardest-numbers value is the **energy-credit stack on the buildings**: deploy ~$700k of energy equipment per site across the portfolio, monetize the federal credits it throws off, and use the **tokenization layer** to lift the separate ~$30M+ §42 LIHTC pipeline from ~77¢ toward 85–90¢ — with the compliance ledger as the origination engine (`STACK-MODEL.md` §0).

---

entries.question: 2. The stacked layers
entries.answer:
Four money systems are stacked on one building set (`COMMERCIAL-WEB.md` §0–§1; `STACK-MODEL.md` §1):

| Layer | Entity | Owns | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Housing** | Frank RE LLC (GPMG) | the buildings, land, make-ready electrical | §42 LIHTC eligible basis; ground-leases to the HoldCo |
| **Equipment** | Blue Star → Loop (resold) | — | straight equipment **purchase** (ownership is credit-critical — NOT charging-as-a-service) |
| **Energy** | Energy HoldCo (non-tribal, Frank-owned) | BESS + chargers | claims **§48E + §30C**, monetizes via §6418 transfer |
| **Compute** | Vegas Edge AI SPV (Alex 51%) | NVIDIA GPUs + cooling | taxable C-corp; takes OpenAI compute revenue |
| **Tokenization** | SAGE / Adinkra | Truth Units → tradable fractions | lifts §42 77¢→85–90¢; securities-gated |

entries.question: 2a. Energy credits (the input store) `[V]`/`⟨?⟩`
entries.answer:
The **§48E storage ITC is the anchor — it has no cliff.** It pays ~40% (30% base + 10% domestic-content) on the BESS capital (~$500k/site) ≈ **~$150k+/site gross, ~$2.25M portfolio** (`STACK-MODEL.md` §2). An energy-community +10% adder would push it to ~50% (~$3.0–3.75M portfolio) but is **unverified for the LV sites** `⟨?⟩`. **§30C chargers** add an L2 floor (~$36k/site) up to a DC ceiling (~$60k/site), but only if energized + permitted + PTO before the hard **6/30/2026 cliff** in an eligible census tract (the original Donna Louise tract already failed → a race-site is needed) `[V]`. Monetization is via a **§6418 transfer at ~85¢** for a deal this size (small ~$1–3M band — *not* the 88–92¢ large/IG band) `[CORRECTED 6/18]`.

> ⚠️ **Three honest corrections to carry (`STACK-MODEL.md` §0, §4):** (1) it is **batteries, not solar** for the §48E earner (no solar capital; 40%, not the 60% solar-paired bonus — though solar IS in scope as a separate ~Jul-4 §48E begin-construction safe harbor); (2) **value ≠ clock** — §48E is bigger but has slack, so the §30C charger deposit must *release first* (~Jun 27, racing 6/30) because §48E can wait and §30C cannot; (3) these are **gross credit, not cash** — realized value nets the §6418 haircut (~8–15¢) *and* the depreciable-basis reduction, so it lands materially below the headline.

entries.question: 2b. LIHTC §42 (the housing layer) `[V]` pipeline / `[D]` lift
entries.answer: Those same buildings are the affordable-housing portfolio. Frank won the majority of Nevada LIHTC awards until the state capped him at one per year. The §42 credits trade OTC at ~77¢ and represent a **~$30M+ re-syndication pipeline** across 12–15 ready properties (`COMMERCIAL-WEB.md` §1.1). **This is a separate monetization track — it is NOT additive to the energy refund.** The thesis is that atomic, unit-level compliance transparency lifts pricing toward 85–90¢ (see 2d).

entries.question: 2c. Edge compute (the capacity store) `[V]`/`[D]`
entries.answer:
Distributed edge compute lives inside the buildings as hidden "sovereign nodes," tapping existing 480V service to skip the multi-year datacenter buildout. Compute capacity is **pre-sold as an IaaS offtake** via a §7701(e)(1) capacity-fee service contract (NOT a $/kWh sale, which would recharacterize as a lease and void the credits). GPU silicon is **NVIDIA / Frontier BYO, owned by the Vegas Edge SPV** — *not* Shaw, who is a pipe/module fabrication + EPC firm, corrected from an earlier hallucination (`COMMERCIAL-WEB.md` §1.5).

> **Size honestly:** the portfolio BESS is **~2.7 MW** (15 sites × ~180 kW); a real compute load is ~400 MW. The battery is a **demand-charge / peak-shaving device on the building's own load**, orthogonal to the GPUs' grid-supplied power — never frame it as "batteries power the AI" (`STORED-COMPUTE-THESIS.md` §1). Aspirational figures ("10–30 MW per US city," "$25M JPMorgan transaction," "11-figure deal value") are founder framing, unbooked `[D]`.

entries.question: 2d. Tokenization (the output store + the lift) `[V]`/`⟨?⟩`
entries.answer: Every compliance event is reduced to an atomic **Truth Unit**, hashed at occurrence, so buyers underwrite at the unit level instead of the building level → audit gets cheap, credits get fractionally tradable, pricing lifts 77¢→85–90¢ (Alex has run this exact playbook on carbon credits). The validation chain: event → Truth Unit → append-only ledger → CPA audit (Molly / ex-Novogradac) → underwriter syndicate → tradable fraction → spot/tokenized market (`COMMERCIAL-WEB.md` §1.4). The deeper architecture is the **Trail Protocol**: a token attests that a computation *happened* — grounded, sealed, consistent — **an event, not an assertion of truth**. That event-not-assertion framing is the legal-neutrality keystone (nothing to be "false" about) and what makes forgeries impossible rather than merely detectable (`STORED-COMPUTE-THESIS.md` §4). **This is where Slater's structuring sits** — he is listed as structuring the instrument ("protect against sharking"), alongside Ed (Energy Web founder, possible validator/architect) (`COMMERCIAL-WEB.md` §1.4).

entries.section: The $100 waterfall (the spine of the cash flow) `[V]`
entries.question: The $100 waterfall (the spine of the cash flow) `[V]`
entries.answer:
OpenAI wires **$100** → Vegas Edge SPV → **$30** to Energy HoldCo (§7701(e) service fee, not $/kWh) + **$70** retained (NVIDIA debt + O&M + equity). HoldCo: **$10** to Frank ($5 ground + $5 infra) + **$20** retained + the credit-refund proceeds, which service the participating debt (`STACK-MODEL.md` §1).

entries.question: 6. Risks & open gates
entries.answer:
**Existential / structural (`STACK-MODEL.md` §6, `COMMERCIAL-WEB.md` §7):**
1. **FEOC / §48E denial** (Chinese-cell BESS) — kills the anchor. Mitigant: Honeywell provenance cert *before* the 5% deposit. `⟨?⟩` #1
2. **§30C sunset** — OBBBA accelerated it to ~6/30/2026; treat the cliff as real (a deposit cannot extend it). `[V]`/`⟨?⟩`
3. **≤1 MW aggregation** — if the portfolio crosses 1 MW and PWA fails, §48E drops 40%→6%: catastrophic. Must confirm. `⟨?⟩`
4. **§385 / §469** — participating-debt → equity recharacterization or passive-credit stranding. (The Jun 16 non-tribal Option B *retired* the §385-tribal existential risk; live risks now §469 + FEOC.) `⟨?⟩`
5. **§7701(e)(4)** — service contract recharacterizing as a lease → voids §48E/§6417. `⟨?⟩`
6. **Recapture** — §48E 20%/yr over 5 years on disposal within the window. `[V]`
7. **Howey/Reves** — tokenization = securities; this gates **every** external token communication. `⟨?⟩` — **this is the gate over Slater's structuring layer.**

**The 7 open gates that must clear before hard numbers (`STACK-MODEL.md` §7):** (1) ~~tribal vs non-tribal scope~~ **RESOLVED** non-tribal Stack-wide; (2) FEOC cell-provenance cert (Honeywell); (3) §48E ≤1 MW aggregation / PWA-exemption; (4) energy-community +10% verification for LV; (5) §30C census-tract + placed-in-service feasibility (site-lock); (6) §6418 transfer rate — a firm JPM/banker quote (placeholder ~85¢); (7) Howey/Reves securities opinion.

entries.section: Puerto Rico Act 60 — Residency & Capital-Gains Memo
entries.question: Puerto Rico Act 60 — Residency & Capital-Gains Memo
entries.answer: _Prep for the Slater visit. Plain-English summary for decision-making — **CPA (Molly) and PR counsel to confirm before any filing.** Not legal/tax advice._

entries.question: Correction up front
entries.answer: The "153-day" figure from the meeting is not the standard test. The Act 60 bona-fide-residence bar is the **183-day presence test** (with alternative pathways). Use 183, not 153.

entries.section: 1. Bona-fide residence (the gate to the benefits)
entries.question: 1. Bona-fide residence (the gate to the benefits)
entries.answer:
You're a bona-fide PR resident for a tax year if you:
- are **present in PR ≥ 183 days** that year (the common path; there are **5 alternative pathways**, incl. **549 days across the current + 2 prior years** with ≥60 days each), AND
- have **no tax home outside PR**, AND
- have **no closer connection** to the US/another country.

entries.section: 2. The capital-gains benefit (and the real deadline)
entries.question: 2. The capital-gains benefit (and the real deadline)
entries.answer:
- Act 60 (Individual Resident Investor) gives **0% PR tax on capital gains, dividends, interest** that **accrue AFTER you become a bona-fide resident**.
- **⏰ Deadline that matters: applications filed BEFORE Jan 1, 2027 lock the 0% rate.** Filed **on/after Jan 1, 2027 → 4%** preferential rate (Act 38-2026). **→ File the decree in 2026.**
- Incentives under the chapter currently expire **Dec 31, 2035**.

entries.question: 3. The clock + the lease
entries.answer:
- The **capital-gains clock starts at residency/decree**, not move-in musings — gains realized **before** you qualify don't get 0%.
- Pre-move (US-period) appreciation has special sourcing rules (often still US-taxable or only partially exempt) — **CPA to model this.**
- **Lease: sign ASAP.** Physical presence + a PR address (lease or purchase) is load-bearing proof of residence; align it with the decree filing.

entries.section: 4. Risks (the IRS is actively scrutinizing Act 60)
entries.question: 4. Risks (the IRS is actively scrutinizing Act 60)
entries.answer:
- IRS has publicly stated it's taking a **hard look**; expect document demands (boarding passes, lease, banking, day-counts).
- **Residency abandonment**: too many days off-island can void status — track day-counts rigorously.
- **Closer-connection / tax-home** tests are where claims fail — keep the center of life in PR.
- **Audit US days-in-country this year** (the open item) to confirm the 183-day path is even achievable for 2026.

entries.question: Next steps
entries.answer:
1. Audit 2026 US days-in-country → is bona-fide residence achievable this year?
2. If yes: **sign a PR lease + file the Act 60 decree before Jan 1, 2027** (ideally now) to lock 0%.
3. CPA models the pre-move appreciation treatment + the day-count plan.

entries.section: The Truth Token — A Tamper-Evident Record That Something Happened
entries.question: The Truth Token — A Tamper-Evident Record That Something Happened
entries.answer:
*External briefing · v1 · June 2026 · prepared for the Slater visit pack.*

> **One line.** Route every event through one chokepoint, seal a content-addressed,
> tamper-evident record that *the event happened* — never that a claim is true — and that single
> object becomes, all at once: stored compute, unified memory, a validator, a dataset, and a
> per-actor proof-of-work trail.

> **The honest boundary, up front.** A Truth Token attests **occurrence, provenance, grounding,
> and chain-integrity**. It does **not** attest **correctness or merit.** We are not saying what is
> true — only that it truly happened. That boundary is what makes the system both legally defensible
> and technically honest, and it is carried on every surface the token touches.

---

entries.section: 1. What it's for — the use cases
entries.question: 1. What it's for — the use cases
entries.answer:
Before the mechanism, here is what it does in practice. Each of these is the *same object* doing a
different job — that is the leverage.

entries.section: 1a. A defensible record that an interaction occurred
entries.question: 1a. A defensible record that an interaction occurred
entries.answer:
When an AI system answers a question, takes an action, or completes an onboarding step, we seal a
record that the event happened, against which corpus it was grounded, and where it sits in the
actor's history. Later, anyone can re-verify that record byte-for-byte. This is the difference
between "we think the model did X" and "here is a tamper-evident proof that X was logged at this
point in this chain."

entries.question: 1b. Onboarding and provenance trails
entries.answer:
A user's interaction history begins at step one and accrues as a sealed, ordered chain — *call
placed → text sent → link tapped → walkthrough complete.* Each step is a block; the chain is the
trail. You can prove the sequence happened, in order, without trusting a mutable database log.

entries.section: 1c. Stored compute / a reusable knowledge asset
entries.question: 1c. Stored compute / a reusable knowledge asset
entries.answer:
Every grounded answer is expensive to produce the first time and free to re-serve. Sealed, content-
addressed, and cross-cited, those answers accumulate into a queryable knowledge graph — a cache
whose marginal cost trends to zero and whose value compounds as it grows.

entries.question: 1d. Attribution and contribution measurement
entries.answer:
Because each block records *who* produced it and *which prior blocks it built on*, an actor's chain
is simultaneously a thinking-ledger and an attribution layer. Contribution is a **computed
derivation over the verified chain** — never a stored score that can be gamed or edited.

entries.question: 1e. A validator and a dataset
entries.answer:
The same sealed records are a clean, provenance-stamped dataset for evaluation and training, and a
validator: "did this answer actually get grounded against the corpus it claims?" is a question the
seal can answer.

---

entries.section: 2. The core insight — relational, not declarative
entries.question: 2. The core insight — relational, not declarative
entries.answer:
A Truth Token records that an **event truly happened**, not that any claim is **true**. This single
reframe is the foundation, and it has three consequences:

- **Neutral.** It attests events and transactions, not assertions — so it sidesteps the
  hate-speech / false-claims liability surface entirely, and it dissolves the common objection that
  "grounding is not the same as correctness." We agree: it isn't. We never assert correctness.
- **Counterfeit-proof.** *No event → no token.* A token derives from a real, logged event; you
  cannot forge a token without forging the underlying event itself. The proof-of-work is simply that
  the work *happened* — the irreducible cost of having actually done it.
- **Honest boundary, everywhere.** A token certifies **provenance + grounding + chain-integrity** —
  never correctness or merit. Every reputation, attribution, or sharing surface carries that
  boundary statement.

---

entries.question: 3. Architecture
entries.answer:
```
event ─▶ record ─▶ seal (per-actor SHA-256 hash chain) ─▶ verify (re-hash + no-event-no-token)
        └──────────────── one chokepoint ────────────────┘
```

Three steps, one chokepoint. Routing every event through a single point is what lets provenance be
produced essentially for free, as a byproduct of infrastructure you already own.

- **The seal** is a per-actor hash chain. Each block's hash commits to the block's content *and* the
  hash of the block before it, recursively back to a genesis hash. Formally:

  ```
  entry_hash = SHA-256( sequence ‖ prev_hash ‖ canonicalJson(scope) ‖ created_at )
  ```

  Because each link folds in the entire prior chain, one 64-character hash certifies a block's full
  content and its complete history. Change any object anywhere and the hash no longer reproduces.

- **The ledger** is content-addressed (keyed by the hash of the query) and supersedable — beliefs
  can be updated, but the history is append-only, so you keep the full record of what was held when.

- **Record → seal → verify.** Recording writes the event; sealing chains it; verifying re-hashes the
  chain and enforces "no event → no token," flagging any of three failure classes:

entries.question: 3. Architecture
entries.answer:
counterfeit (a token with no real event behind it), tampered (a block whose hash no longer
  reproduces), or broken-chain (a gap or reordering in the sequence).

- **The field trail.** An actor's ordered, sealed chain is at once a thinking-ledger and an
  attribution layer. Reputation is computed over the verified chain, never stored.

---

entries.question: 4. The block model — bind, "mine," validate
entries.answer:
The deeper mechanism is worth one section because it is where the leverage comes from.

**The token is a binding.** It is not a claim about content — it is a sealed binding of a **query**
to the **content it produced.** The binding is the fact. It is relational, not declarative.

**The binding is a block.** A block is that bound pair *plus the full relational scope* that gives
the binding meaning: the event that anchors it, the actor's chain position behind it, the other
blocks it depends on, and the corpus it was grounded against. Each of these is an edge of the
relationship, and each is inside the seal:

| Scope field | The relationship it binds |
|---|---|
| query ↔ content | the core binding — this content was produced for this query |
| event anchor | resolves to a real logged event. *No event → no token.* |
| actor + sequence + prev-hash | the block's place in that actor's chain, linked back to genesis |
| dependency edge | which other block this one cites or builds on (attribution) |
| corpus version + capability class | what the answer was grounded against, and its eligibility class |

**"Mining" = sealing and validating the block by hashing its entire scope.** The hash commits to

entries.question: 4. The block model — bind, "mine," validate
entries.answer:
*every object the relationship touches*, so tampering anywhere breaks it. Validating is re-deriving
that scope-hash and proving the anchoring event is real.

The honest framing of "mining": ours is **not** crypto-mining. There is no nonce-hunt, no
artificial difficulty target, no manufactured scarcity. The "difficulty" is the real, irreducible
cost of having actually run the query, grounded the answer, and logged the event. And critically —
**a bigger scope hashed is not "more true."** Hashing more of the relationship makes a block more
*tamper-evident* and richer in *provenance.* It never makes the content more *correct.*

The leverage: "hash the whole relationship" is **one** mechanism doing the job of four — a cache
key, a provenance seal, a tamper-evidence proof, and an attribution-graph node.

entries.question: Worked example (real hashes)
entries.answer:
A user's trail begins with two onboarding blocks. The values below are actual SHA-256 outputs of the
reference implementation, with the timestamp fixed for reproducibility:

```
GENESIS              0000…0000          (the prev_hash of any actor's first block)

Block 1  seq 1  onboarding  query↔content = start-onboarding ↔ welcome+magic-link
         entry_hash = d86b7aea5ff82dd391e4fbecf47de82661d978522e19c4a3ceaf3eafc920c836

Block 2  seq 2  onboarding  depends_on = (a prior block, a cross-actor citation)
         prev_hash  = d86b7aea…   (= Block 1)
         entry_hash = d48b4f2dfc6399fd8b61cf0f5b65b000711e4ceecb0fb7f5198cb1d472523c3f
```

Re-deriving Block 1 from its stored scope reproduces `d86b7aea…` exactly → **valid.** Now tamper a
single field — flip Block 1's content hash:

```
entry_hash(tampered) = bbd336eea808d0cfad836cdd69c7f80f2ad8d7d14590d76d1a4fa60de4b7fc4c   ≠ d86b7aea…
```

The hash no longer reproduces → Block 1 fails (**tampered**). And because Block 2's prev-hash
pointed at the original, **the whole trail downstream breaks too.** You cannot edit any object in a
block's scope without invalidating it and everything chained after it. That is the

entries.question: Worked example (real hashes)
entries.answer:
counterfeit-proofing, demonstrated.

---

entries.section: 5. The three value layers — kept surgically separate
entries.question: 5. The three value layers — kept surgically separate
entries.answer:
This is the most important section for anyone evaluating the upside, and the one place where care is
non-negotiable. There are three distinct layers of value. **Only the third is anything like a
"coin," and conflating them is the entire danger.**

| # | Layer | What it is | Status | Regulatory surface |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Utility / asset** | stored compute + the reusable validated graph (compute saved, marginal cost → 0, a queryable knowledge asset) | **real, here now** | **none** — value-in-use, like a cache or a dataset |
| 2 | **Contribution / reputation** | computed standing over validated work (reuse, citation in/out-degree, validation tier) | **designed** | **none** — *computed, never stored or sold*; "contribution weight," not money |
| 3 | **Speculative / claim** | a **transferable, scarce claim** whose price would float on the expected future utility of the graph | **a labeled moonshot** | **securities + money-transmission — this is the line** |

The intellectually honest part of the thesis: layers 1 and 2 **emergently generate** layer 3. If
contribution is provable and the graph is useful, people will want to trade contribution-claims, and

entries.section: 5. The three value layers — kept surgically separate
entries.question: 5. The three value layers — kept surgically separate
entries.answer:
a market would price them whether or not we build it. So the real question was never *"is there
value"* (there is). It is:

> **Do we convert provable-contribution value into a tradable instrument — taking on the full
> securities and money-transmission burden, and the risk to the neutral core — or do we first make
> the value visible and provable without minting anything tradable?**

entries.question: Where the scarcity actually is
entries.answer:
A common (and previously our own) objection: an answer is infinitely re-derivable, so there is no
scarce underlying and no market is possible. That is true **for one answer** — anyone with the model
can recompute a single query→answer pair. It is **false for the graph.** The scarce thing is the
**validated knowledge graph itself**, in three forms:

- **Path-dependence.** A single hash over every validated, cross-cited block is an unforgeable
  commitment to a *specific history no one else holds.* You can cheaply re-derive one answer; you
  cannot cheaply re-derive a million validated, interlinked, supersession-ordered blocks with their
  provenance.
- **Graph position.** A block that 47 others built on is scarce the way a seminal paper or a
  high-authority page is scarce: there is exactly one, and its value is conferred by the network
  pointing at it.
- **Validation provenance.** A human-validated or consensus-validated block cost real, scarce work to
  certify — and that is not free to reproduce.

So: **no scarcity at the answer level; graph-level scarcity is real.** A market is therefore
*possible and gated*, rather than impossible. This is closer to equity in a compounding knowledge

entries.question: Where the scarcity actually is
entries.answer:
asset than to a mined coin.

---

entries.section: 6. The honest boundary, restated (carry to every surface)
entries.question: 6. The honest boundary, restated (carry to every surface)
entries.answer:
This bears repeating because it is the load-bearing claim of the whole system:

> A Truth Token proves **occurrence + provenance + grounding + chain-integrity** — *that it
> happened, against what it was grounded, in what verified order.* It proves **never correctness or
> merit.** The internal system — memory, evaluation, attribution — is real and validated. A tradable
> market in answer-claims is a labeled, unscheduled moonshot. Grounding is not truth; provenance is
> not correctness; a longer hash is not a more correct answer.

The neutrality is *structural*, not promissory: we assert nothing and sell nothing, so there is
nothing to be wrong about. That structure is precisely what protects the working system — and it is
exactly why the speculative layer must live **beside** the neutral core, never inside it.

---

entries.section: 7. The gates — what stands between here and any tradable instrument
entries.question: 7. The gates — what stands between here and any tradable instrument
entries.answer:
Anyone hearing "there's a coin in this" should hear the gates with equal volume. These are real
gates, not hedges.

entries.question: 7a. Securities law (the Howey / Reves gate)
entries.answer:
A transferable claim on a productive asset, bought with the expectation of profit from *others*
growing the graph, is **presumptively a security.** The Howey test looks at economic reality, not
labels — calling something a "utility token" does not save it; Reves separately captures note-like
instruments. The path exists (a securities reliance opinion → registration or an exemption →
KYC/AML → transfer restrictions) but it is slow, expensive, and **not yet started.** Nothing on the
speculative layer is real until that opinion exists.

entries.question: 7b. Money transmission (the MTS gate)
entries.answer:
Any redemption or transfer involving stored value, or any fiat on/off-ramp, can trigger federal
(FinCEN) registration and state money-transmitter licensing. This is **separate from securities and
separately gated.** It must be cleared independently before any value moves.

entries.section: 7c. The neutrality moat cuts against the coin
entries.question: 7c. The neutrality moat cuts against the coin
entries.answer:
Our legal safety is that we assert nothing and sell nothing. A tradable claim *sells* something and
*invites profit expectation.* You **cannot** make the same object both neutral-by-construction and a
tradable speculative asset. You **can** separate them: a neutral, non-transferable block, plus a
distinct, regulated instrument that merely *references* the graph. That separation is mandatory and
non-negotiable. **The block must stay neutral and non-transferable, or the moat that protects the
working system is gone.**

entries.question: 7d. Centralized now, trustless later
entries.answer:
A fully trustless, decentralized version needs a way to prove the useful-work happened correctly
without re-running it — frontier-scale zero-knowledge or optimistic ML verification, which **does
not yet exist at the scale required.** The graph-scarcity insight relaxes this partly (the Merkle
root is unforgeable regardless), but full trustlessness is a future, not a present.

entries.question: The resolution architecture (non-negotiable shape)
entries.answer:
```
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  NEUTRAL CORE  (the moat — never sold, never transferable)        │
  │  sealed blocks  →  sealed graph  →  one root hash R               │
  │  "events not assertions; no event no token; proves provenance"    │
  └───────────────▲──────────────────────────────┬───────────────────┘
       provenance │ (free, from the chokepoint)   │ references (read-only)
  ┌───────────────┴──────────────────────────────▼───────────────────┐
  │  SEPARATE, COUNSEL-GATED INSTRUMENT LAYER                         │
  │  audited ledger → underwriter → fractional claim / utility credit │
  │  (a securities opinion is REQUIRED before anything here is real)  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Sequence:** *measure first, mint nothing.* Build a read-only meter that makes layers 1 and 2
visible and provable — compute-saved, citation density, validation-weighted contribution, graph
growth over time — with no issuance, no transfer, and no price. That turns "there's value here" from
an assertion into a charted, defensible fact, which is the only honest precondition for ever

entries.question: The resolution architecture (non-negotiable shape)
entries.answer:
discussing an instrument.

---

entries.question: 8. The validation evidence
entries.answer:
The neutral core is not a concept — it is a built, tested system. Representative results from the
test harness:

| Check | Result |
|---|---|
| Cross-language seal parity (two independent implementations) | 300 random vectors (unicode / quotes / emoji) — **0 mismatches** |
| Unit suite (determinism, canonical-JSON, chain cases, store layer, parity) | **12 / 12 pass** |
| Fuzz (random sealed chains, verifier must catch every tamper class) | **5,000 / 0 failures** |
| Soak (60-minute offline fuzz battery) | **57,978 rounds · 463,824 checks · 0 failures** |
| Live end-to-end smoke (record → seal → verify → tamper → heal → cleanup) | **12 / 12 pass** |
| Adversarial review | **9 findings, all fixed and re-validated** |

The seal is proven byte-for-byte identical across two independent implementations, so chains
cross-verify across systems. The neutral, validated substrate is production-grade. The speculative
layer is, again, a labeled moonshot sitting behind the gates in §7.

---

entries.section: 9. A naming flag — "Truth Token" vs. "Trust Token"
entries.question: 9. A naming flag — "Truth Token" vs. "Trust Token"
entries.answer:
One housekeeping item worth surfacing to anyone we share this with: **there is a naming clash to
resolve.** This system is the **"Truth Token."** A separate, unrelated effort (Matt's) uses the name
**"Trust Token."** The two are easy to confuse by ear and in writing, and they are *different
things.* Before any external use of either name, we should deconflict — pick distinct names, or
explicitly scope each — to avoid downstream confusion in conversations, documents, and any future
counsel work. Treat this as an open item, not a settled one.

---

entries.section: 10. Where it stands, in one paragraph
entries.question: 10. Where it stands, in one paragraph
entries.answer:
The neutral core — the block, the seal, the field trail, the validator — is **built, tested, and
working.** It delivers real value today as stored compute, unified memory, a provenance trail, a
validator, and a dataset, with an honest boundary that it proves occurrence and provenance, never
correctness. On top of that substrate sits a genuine but **gated** speculative thesis: the validated
graph (not any answer) is a real, scarce, compounding underlying, and a claim on it could carry
speculative value — which is precisely why it is, in all likelihood, a **security**, and why any
such instrument lives **beside** the neutral block as a separate, counsel-gated thing, never inside
it. We **measure** the value first and **mint** nothing until counsel maps the securities and
money-transmission path.

---

> **The boundary lock (quotable).** The Truth Token (the block) is not a coin and stays neutral and
> non-transferable — that is the legal moat. There **is** a real, scarce, compounding underlying —
> not any answer, but the validated knowledge graph itself: its history, its citation structure, its
> validation provenance. A claim on that graph can carry genuine speculative value — which is exactly

entries.section: 10. Where it stands, in one paragraph
entries.question: 10. Where it stands, in one paragraph
entries.answer:
> why it is, in all likelihood, a **security**, and why any instrument lives **beside** the neutral
> block, behind counsel and money-transmission gates, never inside it. We attest that things
> happened. We do not attest that they are true.

*Prepared as an external, shareable briefing. Any securities-, money-transmission-, or
identity-facing use requires counsel review. The "Truth Token" / "Trust Token" naming should be

entries.question: Jun-18 research corrections (read these first) `[CORRECTED 6/18]`
entries.answer:
From the full-stack + kinetic research sweep (`docs/deals/sources/research/`; synth in `docs/deals/INTEGRATION-MAP.md`):
- **§6418 transfer ≈ 85¢** for our small (~$1–3M) deal — **not 88–92¢** (that's the large/IG band).
- **Solar IS in scope** (the ~Jul-4 §48E begin-construction safe harbor) — the "standalone BESS, no solar" framing below is **superseded**; solar is the §48E earner.
- **Storage = a fork:** lithium (does the demand-charge shave) vs **kinetic/flywheel** (FEOC-/fire-clean UPS, can't do the multi-hour shave) → **complement/hybrid**; CapEx kinetic ~$1,000–1,500/kWh vs lithium $300–800. See `energy-spv/KINETIC-VS-LITHIUM.md`.
- ⚠️ **The $2,600 demand-charge shave may evaporate** — a ~180 kW meter likely sits on **LGS-1 (flat $5.48/kW, no TOU window)**; confirm the schedule (Kyle) before crediting arbitrage value.
- **§6417 bridge** = state climate banks / CDFIs (recourse-on-DSCR), **not** the 90% §6418-bridge market; the 90% LTV / 18-mo assumption is rich/short.
- **100¢ entity = CDPCN (501(c)(4)), not SNRHA** (Hoggard/Bryan). **§30C = $0** (dead); **§179D ~$2.50–5/sqft** is the efficiency lifeline.

entries.question: 0. The one-line thesis
entries.answer:
Deploy ~$700k of energy equipment per site across 15 Las Vegas affordable-housing buildings; monetize
**~$150k+ of §48E credit per site** (the anchor — **standalone BESS, no solar in scope**) plus
cliff-bound §30C upside; repay refund-swept participating debt; capture marginal compute revenue off
the $100 waterfall; and let the tokenization layer lift §42 LIHTC pricing from 77¢→85–90¢ — with the
compliance ledger as the origination engine. **The §48E credit is paid on ~40% of the *whole BESS
capital deployed* — far bigger than the charger credit — so the battery (BESS) play is the load-bearing
prize. But the §30C charger deposit must *release first* (it races the only hard cliff, 6/30); the
§48E deposit has slack (~Jul 4, FEOC threshold). Value says §48E; clock says §30C-deposit-first — they
run in parallel.**

entries.question: 4. The dollar model — scenarios (portfolio, illustrative)
entries.answer:

**The deposit thesis — stress-tested verdict: PARTIALLY SUPPORTED (high confidence).** The §48E
credit is **~40% of the ~$500k BESS capital ≈ $150k+/site** (gross), vs the §30C **L2 floor ~$36k/site**
(~20 ports × ~$1,800; the DC "~$60k" needs the supply-risky DC unit). Per capital-efficiency, **§48E

base + rate.
- **Value ≠ clock.** §48E has **no cliff** + slack (the ~Jul 4 deposit only fixes the FEOC threshold),

entries.section: 6. Stress test — what breaks it, and how sensitive
entries.question: 6. Stress test — what breaks it, and how sensitive
entries.answer:
**Sensitivities (impact on portfolio refund):**
- §6418 rate 88→92¢: ±~$45k. **EC adder +10%**: +~$0.75–1.5M (binary, unverified). **§30C race**:
  +~$0 to +$0.9M (low-confidence). **≤1 MW aggregation**: if it crosses 1 MW and PWA fails, §48E
  40%→6% — **catastrophic** (must confirm). **Compute runtime**: swings the $70-side equity, not the
  credit.
**Existential / structural risks:**
1. **FEOC / §48E denial** (Chinese-cell BESS) — kills the anchor. Mitigant: Honeywell provenance cert
   *before* the 5% deposit. `⟨?⟩` #1
2. **§30C sunset** — OBBBA accelerated it to ~6/30/2026 (Gemini brief missed this; treat the cliff as
   **real** — confirm w/ CPA). `[V]`/`⟨?⟩`
3. **§385/§469** (participating debt → equity recharacterization / passive-credit stranding). `⟨?⟩`
4. **§7701(e)(4)** (service contract → lease). `⟨?⟩`
5. **Recapture** (disposal within window claws back). `[V]`
6. **Howey/Reves** (tokenization = securities) — gates all token comms. `⟨?⟩`
**No double-counts:** carbon ≠ RECs (bifurcate the PPA); carbon additionality subtracts realized
§30C/§48E; LIHTC ~$30M is a separate track, not added to the energy refund.

entries.section: Part C — Fit rubric (so scores are defensible, not vibes)
entries.question: Part C — Fit rubric (so scores are defensible, not vibes)
entries.answer:
Score each filled seat 0–5: **1** = wrong peg · **2** = real gaps, needs backfill · **3** = workable · **4** = strong · **5** = ideal.
Score on three dimensions, take the floor:
1. **Capability match** — do they cover the seat's `required capabilities`?
2. **Compartment-safety** — can they be kept need-to-know inside this seat's tier? (a leak risk caps the score)
3. **Clock-fit** — can they hit the gate's deadline?

A `critical-path` seat at fit < 3, or VACANT, is a **red flag** — surface it in STATE and the reckoning.

entries.question: The Deal at 30,000 Feet
entries.answer:
*The single top-of-tree view. Streamlines the four 2026-06-25 document sets (energy, bonds, FEMA, money out) plus the ledger that prices them, into one altitude so we can drill down from here. 2026-06-25 · Owner: Alex.*

> **v0, not investment / legal / tax advice.** Every load-bearing dollar is gated to CPA (Molly / Novogradac), bond counsel, securities counsel, NHD, and David Wadsworth's field walk before anyone acts. Confidence tags: `[C]` confirmed · `[G]` grounded (cited, not counsel-blessed) · `[A]` assumed (a walk/quote converts it) · `[?]` open · `[D]` founder-framing (directionally real, numerically soft).

entries.section: 0. The whole deal in one paragraph
entries.question: 0. The whole deal in one paragraph
entries.answer: Frank Hawkins controls ~16 Las Vegas affordable-housing buildings (17 parcels, Owens sold; ~1,100 units; 14 entities) and three of the four seats that make a housing deal work: development (CDPCN, his 501(c)(4)), construction (NCP, his GC), and operations (GPM). The one open seat is the capital and structuring quarterback, the role Craig and Alex fill. On those same buildings we stack instruments: re-syndication proceeds (LIHTC), energy credits, energy arbitrage, AI compute revenue, FEMA safe-haven, and a tokenized credit rail. The four document sets are four of those layers, and one more thing sits across all of them: an auditable **ledger** that lifts the price we get when we sell the credits. The frame that ties it together: the **LIHTC engine is the acquisition flywheel** (the only instrument that adds buildings), the **energy + compute layer is the high-multiple rent check**, **FEMA is a Phase-2 hardening rebate**, "**money out**" is what the machine throws off once it runs, and the **ledger is the spread amplifier** that makes every credit sell closer to par. Credits are the down-payment; the AI servers are the rent; the ledger is why the down-payment is bigger.

entries.section: 2. The ledger: how we get a better spread on the credits
entries.question: 2. The ledger: how we get a better spread on the credits
entries.answer:
This is the piece that was missing, and it is a lever, not a footnote. The credits (the §42 LIHTC equity and the §48E energy transfer) are the "current instrument" we sell. Frank's specific deal sells them at a discount because it is small (under $5M energy face), unrated, first-time, and affordable-housing-complex. The market docks a first-time / unrated seller **2 to 4 cents** on the dollar, and small §6418 transfers sit at **~83 to 89¢ gross (~80 to 83¢ net)** versus the 93 to 94¢ utility-scale standard.

The fix: the same append-only compliance ledger Frank's tenant platform already runs (recerts, lease execution, income checks, rent ledger, every event hashed and bound to its governing HUD/IRS citation) emits unit-level **Truth Units**. That auditable provenance is exactly what a credit desk underwrites. Run the validation chain and the price moves up:

> operational event → Truth Unit (hashed, cited) → append-only ledger → CPA attestation (Molly / Novogradac) → Lloyd's-style underwriter wrap (tax-credit insurance) → validated, fractional credit.

Three ways it lifts the price:
1. **Unit-level underwriting instead of building-level.** Buyers normally price §50(a) recapture risk across the whole building or portfolio (expensive diligence). Auditing at the unit level makes diligence cheap, so the credit transacts at a tighter spread.

entries.section: 2. The ledger: how we get a better spread on the credits
entries.question: 2. The ledger: how we get a better spread on the credits
entries.answer:
2. **It substitutes for a credit rating.** Frank's HoldCo has no public rating, so it loses the investment-grade premium (~2 to 4¢). Non-falsifiable proof of statutory compliance (prevailing-wage labor logs, census-tract qualification) offsets the missing rating and recaptures that penalty.
3. **Strongest-leg bundling.** Package the energy (§48E/§30C), housing (§42), and the compute offtake into one bundle whose price floats on the strongest, most-liquid leg, dragging the small, illiquid energy fractions up.

**What it is worth (honest):**
- LIHTC: the often-quoted "77¢ → 85-90¢" is mis-anchored. Primary LIHTC already pays ~85¢, so the real win is closing a small-deal / distressed discount and defending the high end, not beating the market. On a $50M deal with ~$18.6M of 10-yr credits, each cent ≈ $186k; defending ~$0.88 to $0.90 instead of slipping to ~$0.82 to $0.83 is roughly **$1.1 to $1.5M of extra equity on one deal.**
- Energy §6418: target ~88 to 90¢ gross (~82 to 88¢ net) instead of ~83 to 85¢. On ~$2.5M of portfolio energy credits, each cent ≈ $25k, so ~$125 to $150k.

**The hard gates (do not skip):**

entries.section: 3. Bond deal vs tax-credit sale (and what is new vs what Frank already runs)
entries.question: 3. Bond deal vs tax-credit sale (and what is new vs what Frank already runs)
entries.answer:
**Frank already sells tax credits.** His running business sells the §42 housing credits to bank investors, **RBC Community Investments** and **JPMorgan / Chase**, who take ~99.99% of the partnership for 10 to 15 years and use the credits against their own taxes. RBC is live right now on the 1501 Phase 2 / Hoggard solar diligence (Kevin Jones); the ledger was demoed to Chase on Jun 11. That is the engine already turning.

**What is new in what we propose** is not "sell credits," it is the 4% tax-exempt **bond** resyndication structure wrapped around it, plus the **energy-credit (§6418)** and **ledger** layers. So be precise about two instruments people conflate:

| | The bond | The tax-credit sale |
|---|---|---|
| **What it is** | Debt. The project borrows (e.g., ~$13 to 18M) | Equity. We sell the 4% LIHTC (e.g., ~$9 to 16M) |
| **The "4%"** | Roughly the tax-exempt interest COUPON we pay | The credit RATE (≈4% of basis/yr for 10 yrs) we sell |
| **Direction of money** | We receive it, then repay it | We receive it, never repay it |
| **Cost** | Debt service every year (low-to-mid single digits, tax-exempt), at ~1.15x DSCR, secured by a mortgage | None: the buyer's return IS the credit + tax losses |

entries.section: 3. Bond deal vs tax-credit sale (and what is new vs what Frank already runs)
entries.question: 3. Bond deal vs tax-credit sale (and what is new vs what Frank already runs)
entries.answer:
| **Its hidden job** | Financing ≥25% of basis is what UNLOCKS the 4% credit as-of-right (the OBBBA test) | The cheapest capital in the stack; fills ~25 to 35% of cost |
| **Who holds it** | A bond fund, or a bank in a private placement | A CRA-motivated bank: RBC, Chase, BofA |

The two are **not** an either/or. The bond is the key that unlocks the credit, and in many 4% structures the credit equity from RBC/Chase pays down the construction bond at conversion. (This deal models the bond as a ~$13M permanent mortgage WITH ~$9M of separate LIHTC equity, both in the stack.) You use both: maximize the cheap credit equity, and size the bond to the minimum that clears the 25% test and that NOI can service.

**On "sell the credits to the bondholders vs to Chase":** that is a pricing question, and it is why the buyer's identity matters.
- A generic bondholder (a bond fund) usually cannot use the LIHTC at all (no CRA motive, limited tax appetite), so they are not the natural credit buyer.
- A CRA-motivated bank (Chase, RBC) pays a premium, upper-$0.80s to $0.90+ (best deals near par), because they get Community Reinvestment Act credit plus the relationship. The CRA premium runs ~5 to 30¢ over a non-CRA "economic" buyer.

entries.section: 3. Bond deal vs tax-credit sale (and what is new vs what Frank already runs)
entries.question: 3. Bond deal vs tax-credit sale (and what is new vs what Frank already runs)
entries.answer:
- So selling the credit to Chase/RBC rather than to a plain bondholder is worth real money: on ~$18.6M of credits, the gap between a low-$0.80s economic bid and a ~$0.90 CRA bid is **~$1.3 to $1.5M of equity on a single $50M deal.** The ledger (§2) is the lever that defends the high end of that band for Frank's small, unrated deal.

**The bottom line on the finance difference:** the bond is money we rent at low-single-digit interest and pay back; the tax-credit sale is money we keep. Per dollar, the credit equity is far cheaper, which is why the whole structure maximizes credit equity (sold to a CRA bank at the best price the ledger can defend) and uses only as much bond debt as the 25% test and the NOI require.

---

entries.question: 4. The money picture, unified
entries.answer:
**Money OUT (what we spend):**
- Energy infra ~$0.8-1.0M/site net of §48E, ~$12.6M fleet `[G/P]` (project-financed).
- Server capex ~$283M fleet, ~95% of all capital, but **carried by the offtake + NVIDIA financing, staged per node, not day-one cash** `[D]`.
- Bridge debt ~$700k/site @ 8.5% to span the §6418 refund gap into late 2027 `[G]`.
- Near-term real cash: §30C charger deposit (Jun 27), Molly / Novogradac / counsel fees, FEMA 25% match (~$90-250k/site) **only if** FEMA is pursued.
- LIHTC resyndication TDC ~$40-50M per deal, but project-financed (bond + LIHTC equity + NAHA + seller note); the sponsor's real exposure is the deferred fee + the guaranty stack (net worth ~$6M / $2.5M liquid).

**Money OUT in the other sense (what the machine yields):**
- Hard recurring today: **~$45-60k/site/yr** (mostly solar avoided-cost) `[A]`.
- The bet: **~$425-850k/site/yr** compute service fee to the energy HoldCo at ~425 kW (~2.8x at a 480V hi-rise) `[D]`.
- One-time: §48E ~$150-204k/site, §30C ~$0-60k/site, FEMA ~$250-350k/site (Phase-2). Credits and FEMA are capital events, not a stream.
- Per building worth: **~$0 standalone (a wash)** / **~$2-5M as a compute node** (up to ~$8M all-in incl. Alex's 51% of the SPV) `[D]`.

entries.question: 4. The money picture, unified
entries.answer:
- **The ledger adds ~$1.3-1.5M of credit equity per $50M bond deal** by defending the high end of the credit price (§2). Upside, not in the base.

**The valuation, floor and ceiling:**
- **Bankable floor (high 8 to 9 figures), grounded:** the LIHTC developer/operator platform + the credit harvesting + the controlled real estate.
- **Ceiling (10 to 11 figures), the founder thesis:** the welded multi-instrument Stack at national scale, which lives or dies on the AI compute + tokenization layers landing.
- **The one-liner:** a solid nine-figure floor on housing + credits, with a credible path to a ten-to-eleven-figure ceiling on compute, and the LIHTC engine is the multiplier that unlocks the ceiling because it is the only lever that scales the building count.

**At RISK (gated on a date or a cert):**
- §30C (~$76k portfolio): dies **Jun 30** if not placed in service (likely unreachable, no safe harbor).
- §48E (~$2.25M+ anchor): denied entirely on FEOC (Chinese-cell) without a written Honeywell cert; craters 40% → 8% if any site crosses 1 MW with PWA unmet.
- Credit PRICE (~2-4¢, ~$0.5-1.5M/deal): the first-time/unrated-seller penalty, which the ledger is built to defend against.

entries.question: 6. The two keystones (what unlocks everything)
entries.answer:
Everything above swings on two unlocks. Clear these two and the model turns from assumption into quotes:

1. **David Wadsworth's field walk** (energy + money-out). Every dollar in the compute model rests on the "~2,000 A service, residents just sipping" assumption. Measured common-area load today is only ~20-23 kW. David's walk reads the actual main, the resident load, and the Rule 9 upsize timeline. It is the single input that converts the whole node model from a model into a quote, per building. **Overdue.**
2. **Name the property / pick the bundle** (bonds). The LIHTC engine is blocked until Frank's team shares placed-in-service dates, locations (in-city or not), unit counts, and 3-yr financials, so we can pick the 4-8 buildings for the 2026 4% bundle and reserve the best scorers for the 2027 9% round. Candidates flagged: Sarann Knight (resync) vs Donna Louise 2 (new-build §45L, not a resync).

Secondary structural gates: the guaranty / balance-sheet stack, the multi-nonprofit per-principal aggregation question (the dial on the 9% engine), the FEOC / Honeywell cell-provenance cert (the §48E kill-switch), and the securities opinion (the gate on anything tokenized in the ledger layer).

entries.question: 7. The honest read
entries.answer:
- **It is a compute deal in an energy-credit jacket, acquired by a LIHTC flywheel, priced by a ledger.** Energy infra is ~$1M/site; the servers are $12-37M/site (~95% of capital). The credits de-risk the cheap layer; the compute rent and the LIHTC platform carry the value; the ledger improves the price on the way out the door.
- **The floor is real and bankable; the ceiling is a bet.** Housing + credits + controlled RE is a genuine nine-figure enterprise that executes today. The ten-to-eleven-figure number lives entirely on the compute + tokenization layers landing at national scale.
- **Recurring vs one-time is the discipline.** Only the compute offtake pays year after year. Credits and FEMA are one-time capex offsets; the ledger lift is a one-time price improvement on those credits. Do not model any of them as income, and bank $0 of FEMA and $0 of the tokenization lift in the base case.
- **Two people convert the whole thing:** David (does the capacity physically exist) and Frank's team (name the bundle). Everything else is second-order until those two land.

---

entries.question: 8. The operating model (capacity arbitrage)
entries.answer:
How the power physically flows, the operating concept under the energy layer:
- We take over each building's energy (master-meter) and give residents a fixed **free** baseline (Rule-18 clean).
- We push the building's grid allocation to the **servers** as the dominant load; residents float on **battery + solar** through peaks.
- Compute runs as **interruptible load**: when the grid or the residents need power, the servers throttle and the battery + solar carry the gap. That interruptibility is also what keeps the dispatch arm's-length (a credit-structuring point, see §9, gate 3).
- Net: each building becomes a **self-balancing node**, residents always served, spare capacity monetized as compute, the battery the shock absorber. `[G]` (memory `energy-capacity-arbitrage-operating-model`)

entries.section: 9. The entity stack + the existential gates
entries.question: 9. The entity stack + the existential gates
entries.answer:
The legal architecture, and what can void the credits (all counsel-gated):

```
Frank RE LLC            buildings, land, §42 basis; ground-leases to ↓
  └─ Energy HoldCo      owns §48E BESS + §30C chargers; monetizes via §6418 (~85¢)
        └─ Compute SPV  owns the GPUs + cooling; pays HoldCo a §7701(e) SERVICE fee (not $/kWh)
              └─ Token Transactor   the instruments rail; design-only until a securities opinion
```

The five existential gates, any one can kill the credits:
1. **FEOC** (Chinese-cell content) → §48E denied without a written Honeywell cert. ☠️
2. **§385** debt-vs-equity → participating debt recharacterizes to equity.
3. **§7701(e)(4)** related-party → an offtaker with dispatch control → lease → §168(h) taint → credit voided (dispatch stays **interruptible-only and arm's-length**).
4. **§48E ≤1 MW aggregation** → cross 1 MW with PWA unmet → 40% craters to 6-8%.
5. **Howey/Reves** → any token/redeem language is presumptively a security; a securities opinion gates all token comms.

The whole structure exists to keep these gates clear; counsel reliance on each is the unlock.

entries.question: 10. Money in (the capital + counterparties)
entries.answer:
Who funds it, to complement §4's money-out:
- **§6418 transfer buyer** (structured-credit desk, JPM/Chase): cash for the energy credits ~85¢, mid-late 2027.
- **The bridge** (~$700k/site @ 8.5%, refund-swept; potential JPM bridge): spans the gap to the 2027 refund.
- **LIHTC equity** (RBC/Chase): the housing-credit equity into the bond deals.
- **NAHA** (Nevada): the state subordinate gap loan.
- **NVIDIA / Frontier**: equipment financing for the servers (the ~$283M, staged per node).
- **Delphi / token-led raise**: the instruments-rail capital ("finance the token, not the company").
- **Lloyd's**: tax-credit insurance wrapping the validated credits (the ledger layer). `[G/?]` every figure CPA/banker-gated.

entries.question: In one line
entries.answer:
**Your buildings already have the heavy power that AI servers need. We use federal tax credits to put
solar + batteries on them — the government pays back 30–50% as cash — then run AI servers off that
power. The AI servers pay rent, forever. The credits are the down payment; the AI servers are the
rent check.**

---

entries.section: Why YOUR buildings (this is the moat)
entries.question: Why YOUR buildings (this is the moat)
entries.answer:
- They already have **480-volt industrial power.** Most properties don't.
- Everyone else building AI capacity is stuck in a **3–4 year line** waiting on the utility for power.
  **You skip the line.**
- AI has to sit **close to the user** to be fast — Las Vegas buildings are the right spot.
- **Almost nobody else can do this deal.** It only works on buildings like yours.

entries.question: The money, simply
entries.answer:
1. We install **solar + batteries** (+ chargers where they fit) on your buildings.
2. **Federal credits pay back 30–50% of the cost — as cash** (~90¢ on the dollar).
3. That equipment powers **AI servers** — roughly **one server room per building (~180 kW)**.
4. The AI customer pays for compute → that money flows down to **power rent + building rent.**
   **Your buildings earn on every dollar.** (AI companies are openly paying for this much compute
   right now — the demand is real.)

> **The battery does two jobs with one asset:** a server room would rack up **~$2,600/month** in
> utility demand charges — the battery wipes that out by charging cheap at night. So it **earns the
> credit AND kills the bill.** That's why we grab every credit we can.

entries.question: What each piece pays back
entries.answer:
| Equipment | Pays back |
|---|---|
| ☀️ **Solar** | ~40–50% |
| 🔋 **Batteries** (any type — even flywheel/storage) | ~40–50% |
| 🔌 **EV chargers** | ~30% (up to $100k per plug) |
| 🏠 **Efficiency upgrades** | $2,500–5,000 per apartment + a tax deduction |

> **A no-fire "battery" option (for the server rooms):** there's a kind of storage called a **flywheel** — it holds
> power in a spinning steel wheel, no chemicals. It **can't catch fire**, lasts ~30 years, and **sidesteps the
> China-parts problem** that can disqualify a normal battery's credit — so it's excellent as **backup power for the
> server room**. The catch: it can't hold power for *hours*, so for **shaving the monthly power bill** you still want a
> normal (lithium) battery. Likely answer: **use both** — flywheel for the servers, lithium for the bill. *(We're
> double-checking the exact power-bill math first — it may be smaller than we thought.)*

entries.question: The clock — three deadlines
entries.answer:
| Piece | Deadline | Real status |
|---|---|---|
| 🔌 **Chargers** | **June 30** — installed **and** turned on | 🔴 Probably too late for new ones — only if already mid-install |
| ☀️ **Solar** | **~July 4** — just have to **START** (spend 5%) | 🟢 **The live one — start it now** (the rule was just restored in court; could be appealed, so don't wait), locks the credit to ~2030 |
| 🎁 **+20% bonus** (low-income) | **Aug 7** — apply for the allocation | 🟡 Affordable housing qualifies — worth chasing |
| 🔋 **Batteries** | No rush (years) | 🟢 The patient money |

> Honest read: the **charger** race is basically over unless something's already in the ground. The
> real prize is **solar + battery + the 20% bonus** — and solar's start-by-July-4 window is the move.

entries.section: The smart play on your Housing-Authority buildings (Hoggard, Bryan)
entries.question: The smart play on your Housing-Authority buildings (Hoggard, Bryan)
entries.answer:
We first thought these were the *hard* ones because of the government partner. **It's the opposite.**
A government partner can take the credit as **straight Treasury cash (100¢, not 90¢)** *and* unlock
the **extra 20% bonus**. So **Hoggard & Bryan may be our best sites, not our most limited.** We just
need to confirm who owns the equipment and structure it right (Molly's call).

2. **Hoggard & Bryan:** does the Housing Authority **own the equipment**, or just sponsor the housing?

4. **Green-light two moves before the clock:** start the **solar (5%) by ~July 4**, and **apply for

entries.section: The bigger picture (if Frank asks "so what is this, really?")
entries.question: The bigger picture (if Frank asks "so what is this, really?")
entries.answer:
Three income streams welded onto one set of buildings: **AI compute rent + the affordable-housing you
already run + clean-energy credits.** The credits make the power nearly free; the free power runs the
AI; the AI pays rent. **Your portfolio becomes the host for an AI network — built with the
government's money.**

Deploy edge-AI compute into ~15 existing Las Vegas affordable-housing buildings, using

entries.question: Entity structure (current: non-tribal)
entries.answer:
- **Non-tribal, Frank-owned Energy HoldCo** owns the energy equipment (§48E BESS + §30C
  chargers). Decided **D-2026-06-16-01** (Blue Star entity), extended **Stack-wide in
  D-2026-06-17-02** (the Chickasaw / tribal entity is dropped everywhere). Why: it removes
  the **§385** debt-vs-equity existential risk and the tribal-validation gate. A worked deal
  at ~90¢ beats a stranded one at 100¢.
- Assets are compartmentalized: one real-estate landlord LLC plus isolated operating boxes,
  to insulate the credit, compute, and housing interests from each other.
- **Hard gate:** the HoldCo must exist and hold an EIN **before the first Blue Star invoice**
  (owner: corporate counsel).
- ⚠️ **Corpus staleness:** the NLM briefing's "$100 waterfall to **Tribal** HoldCo / §6417
  100¢" is the **superseded** structure. The waterfall *mechanics* (SPV → HoldCo → RE LLC
  compute-revenue split) still hold; the owner is the **non-tribal** HoldCo and monetization
  is §6418 transfer (below).

entries.question: Monetization
entries.answer:

  dollar. The decision log carries ~88-92¢ / ~90¢ as the working assumption
  (D-2026-06-16-01, D-2026-06-17-02); the Jun-18 market research refined small-deal
  (~$1-3M, unrated C&I) pricing to **~85¢**. The real number is an **open gate**: the
  banker's live §6418 quote (owner: banker / JPM-Chase).
- **Optional 100¢ lane (open):** the two governmental-housing sites (Hoggard, Bryan) can
  pursue **§6417 direct pay @100¢** via a **§761(a)** energy-asset co-ownership, the
  applicable entity being a government housing authority (named in the corpus as SNHA; newer
  research points to SNRHA plus Frank's 501(c)(4) **CDPCN**, so the exact entity is a confirm
  item). This is **not** the dropped tribal path (a governmental instrumentality carries no
  §385 risk). Gated on Molly confirming applicable-entity status + the §761(a) structure.

entries.section: Existential risks (each can independently zero the credits)
entries.question: Existential risks (each can independently zero the credits)
entries.answer:
- **FEOC material assistance:** Chinese-cell provenance in the BESS denies §48E entirely.
  Mitigate with a written Honeywell cert before payment, or a US flywheel.
- **§7701(e)(4) recharacterization:** the related-party AI-SPV off-taker could recast the
  power / service contract as a lease, making the BESS §168(h) tax-exempt-use property and
  voiding §48E + direct pay. Mitigate with a capacity fee + avoided-cost bonus (not $/kWh),
  independent O&M, and interruptible-only signaling.
- **§48E ≤1 MW aggregation:** if the IRS deems the rollout "integrated operations," sites
  aggregate >1 MW, losing the PWA exemption (30% → 6%) and tripping the domestic-content
  direct-pay cliff.
- **§469 passive-activity / at-risk stranding:** credits on the taxable Frank-owned HoldCo
  must be structured so they do not strand on Frank individually.

entries.question: 1 · The one-line thesis
entries.answer: Frank's ~15 LV affordable-housing buildings already carry the **480V industrial power AI needs and skip the 3–4-yr interconnect queue**; federal credits fund **~40% of the battery as a near-cash refund**; the batteries earn the credit while killing the building's demand charge as AI servers pay rent; and the **housing-compliance ledger that makes the deal auditable also lifts the housing tax credit.** `[V]` structure / `[D]` the lift. The asset is the building; the *living ledger* turns separate tax positions into one underwriteable stack.

entries.section: 2 · The moat — the building, not the technology
entries.question: 2 · The moat — the building, not the technology
entries.answer:
480V industrial power most properties lack `[V]`; skips the 3–4-yr interconnect queue `[V]`; edge AI wants LV proximity `[V]`; almost no one else can assemble it `[V]`.
**Honest caveat:** the moat is the *premise of heavy service*, not proven per-site headroom — measured common-area service is **~20–23 kW** vs a **~180 kW target** (~8×), so a **Rule-9 upgrade is the binding constraint** (ledger D1; per-site MW open). And **the layers are not welded by electrons** (`STORED-COMPUTE-THESIS.md`): a ~2.7 MW portfolio BESS cannot power a ~400 MW GPU load — the battery is a **demand-charge/peak-shaving device**, GPUs are grid-supplied; the weld is **ownership + financing + provenance, not power flow.** That version survives counsel.

entries.answer:

**Version:** v0.1 · 2026-06-17 · Companion: *Risk & Mitigation* (forthcoming). Validation basis:
the per-claim ledger (`docs/deals/energy-spv/VALIDATION-LEDGER.md`), grounded against primary law in

---

entries.question: 1. Executive summary
entries.answer:
Affordable-housing portfolios are an unusually good substrate for federal clean-energy credits: many
small rooftops in low-income census tracts, a sponsor that is often a government or tax-exempt entity,
and an existing LIHTC capital structure. Layered correctly, a single building can carry **EV-charger,
battery-storage, and solar credits** — and, depending on *who owns the energy assets*, those credits
can be monetized as a **transferable credit (~90¢)** or as **direct cash from Treasury (100¢)**, with
an additional **+20% solar bonus** available in qualified low-income residential buildings.

The thesis is not "stack every credit on every building." It is **match each building to the right
monetization door**, respect three hard OBBBA deadlines, and — critically — **carry a validation
discipline** so every number is grounded in primary law and confirmed by counsel before it is banked.

---

entries.question: 2. The opportunity
entries.answer:
| Feature of affordable housing | Why it matters for credits |
|---|---|
| Located in low-income census tracts | §30C eligibility; §48E(h) low-income bonus territory |
| Many roofs < 5 MW each | qualifies for the small-facility 30% base and the low-income bonus |
| Government / tax-exempt sponsor common | unlocks **§6417 direct pay** (100¢, no tax appetite needed) ✅ |
| Existing LIHTC partnership | a known structure to layer onto (and to firewall against double-dip) |
| Resident EV/charging + resilience needs | real use case for chargers + battery, not credit-chasing |

---

entries.question: 3. The credit stack (illustrative)
entries.answer:
Five federal credits can ride the same building, but **they are not additive on the same asset** —
energy property (§48E/§30C) and the housing (§45L/§179D/§42) sit in different owners, and §6417 vs the
§42 basis is one-lane-per-asset. ✅

| Credit | Asset | Illustrative rate | Key gate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **§48E** | battery storage (BESS) | **30% base**; sub-1 MW is PWA-exempt (auto-30%); **+10%** domestic content; **+10%** energy community | no placed-in-service cliff; 5-yr recapture ✅ |
| **§48E** | solar generation | 30% base **+ up to 20%** low-income bonus → **up to ~50%** | begin construction by the solar deadline; allocation for the bonus ⚠️ |
| **§30C** | EV chargers | **30%** (prevailing-wage-gated; 6% otherwise); **$100k per port** | **hard placed-in-service cliff (below)** ✅ |
| **§45L** | dwelling units | $2,500 / $5,000 per unit — **NARROW: only units first leased/"acquired" 1/1/23–6/30/26** (or post-substantial-rehab); already-occupied stock is out ⚠️ | unit "acquired" + HERS cert by 6/30 |
| **§179D** | commercial space | **~$2.97/sq ft** (PWA, at 25% floor; $5.94 ceiling at ~50%) — **only ≥4-story buildings** (garden-style out) ⚠️ | begin construction / 5% safe harbor by 6/30 |

entries.question: 3. The credit stack (illustrative)
entries.answer:
> **One lane per asset.** If the energy property is owned by an entity taking §6417/§6418 on it, that
> same battery cannot also sit in the LIHTC partnership's §42 eligible basis. The structure must choose
> per asset. ✅ (`01-credit-stack.md`)

---

entries.question: 4. Monetization — the three doors
entries.answer:
This is the heart of the play. *Who owns the energy asset* determines how the credit turns into money.

**Door 1 — §6418 transfer (~85–88¢ for small <1 MW deals; ~88–92¢ is the large/investment-grade band).**
A **taxable owner** sells the credit to a third party for cash. Small projects + non-institutional
sponsors price at the low end. ⚠️ (actual cents quote-dependent — verified vs 2026 market data)

**Door 2 — §6417 direct pay (100¢).** An **"applicable entity"** — a tax-exempt organization, a
government, or an **instrumentality of a state or local government** — elects to receive the credit as
a **direct cash payment from Treasury**, no tax liability required. §6417 expressly covers both **§30C
and §48E**. ✅ *(26 U.S.C. §6417(b)(1),(12); IRS Elective Pay FAQ.)*

**Door 3 — §761(a) co-ownership (the bridge to Door 2).** A **partnership generally cannot elect direct
pay** (the only exceptions are §45Q/§45V/§45X). ✅ Since most affordable-housing deals are partnerships,
direct pay is *not automatic* even when a government entity is a partner. The fix: carve the **energy
property** into an **unincorporated co-ownership owned in part by the applicable entity** and **elect

entries.question: 4. Monetization — the three doors
entries.answer:
out of subchapter K under §761(a)**; each applicable-entity co-owner then makes its **own elective-pay
election on its undivided share.** ✅ *(26 CFR §1.761-2(a)(4)(ii); Reg. Example 1 confirms a 40%
applicable-entity co-owner electing §6417 on its share of solar.)*

```
            Building with a government/tax-exempt sponsor
                              │
        ┌─────────────────────┴─────────────────────┐
   LIHTC housing partnership              Energy-asset co-ownership   ← §761(a) opt-out
   (§42, units — unchanged)               (chargers + battery + solar)
                                                     │
                              ┌──────────────────────┴──────────────────────┐
                     applicable-entity share                    taxable-owner share
                     → §6417 direct pay 100¢                    → §6418 transfer ~90¢
```

**Door-selection rule of thumb:** government/tax-exempt-sponsored buildings → Door 2/3 (100¢);
ordinary taxable-owner buildings → Door 1 (~85–90¢). The premium for getting an asset onto Door 2 is
~**10–15¢ on the credit base**, and via **§6417(d)(2)** the applicable entity claims **without

entries.question: 4. Monetization — the three doors
entries.answer:
§50(b)(3)/(4)(A)(i)** — so the tax-exempt-use haircut is **off** ✅ (verified; *owner-specific* — only
if the entity actually owns its §761(a) share; and the **§45(b)(3) tax-exempt-bond ~15% haircut still
applies** on bond-financed deals — see Risk companion).

---

entries.question: 5. The bonus levers
entries.answer:
- **Domestic content +10%** (§48E) — cost-ratio test against current Treasury tables; documentary. ✅
- **Energy community +10%** (§48E) — fact-specific to the site (coal-closure / brownfield / statistical
  area). ⚠️
- **Low-income communities bonus (§48E(h)) — +10%/+20%.** ✅ **+20%** for a **"qualified low-income
  residential building project,"** for facilities with **< 5 MW (AC)** output — i.e., it rides **solar
  generation, not standalone storage.** Stacked on a 30% base, solar reaches **~50%.** Requires a
  **pre-application + capacity allocation** before placed-in-service. *(IRS Clean Electricity LIC Bonus
  program; 26 CFR §1.48(e)-1.)* ✅

> **Precision that matters:** the +20% does **not** apply to a standalone battery (the Category-3 bonus
> is generation-only). The bonus is a **solar** lever. ✅ Mixing this up overstates the battery case.

---

entries.question: 6. The OBBBA clocks
entries.answer:
Three federal deadlines govern timing; missing one forfeits that credit, not the others.

| Lever | Action required | Hard date | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **§30C chargers** | **placed in service & operational** | **Jun 30, 2026** | no safe harbor; direct pay does **not** move it ✅ |
| **§48E solar** | **begin construction** (5% safe harbor or physical work) | **~Jul 4, 2026** | locks a placed-in-service runway to ~2030; otherwise must be in service by **Dec 31, 2027** ✅ |
| **§48E(h) bonus** | **secure a capacity allocation** | program window (rolling) | hold the allocation *before* solar is placed in service ✅ |
| **§48E battery** | begin construction | (long runway) | storage is **not** on the solar/wind acceleration ✅ |

**Implication:** the **charger** credit is a near-term sprint (often already out of reach for new
installs); the **solar + battery** credits, and the **direct-pay + low-income** value, are where the
runway and the upside concentrate.

---

entries.question: 7. Risk & mitigation (summary)
entries.answer:
Full treatment in the companion. The load-bearing risks, each of which can independently impair a credit:

1. **Applicable-entity status (the keystone for Door 2).** Direct pay requires the claimant *actually*
   be an applicable entity. The *category* (government instrumentalities) is settled law; whether a
   **specific** sponsor qualifies is a **counsel/PLR-level determination.** ⚠️ *(This is the single
   most load-bearing assumption in the direct-pay leg.)*
2. **FEOC / foreign-entity "material assistance."** From 2026, §48E can be **denied** if the project
   receives material assistance from a prohibited foreign entity — vet the **battery/solar supply
   chain** (cells especially). ✅ Mitigation: written cell-provenance certification before commitment.
3. **Tax-exempt-use property (§50(b)/§168(h)).** If a *taxable* owner claims while a tax-exempt entity
   uses the property, the credit can be haircut/denied — which is precisely *why* Door 2 (the exempt
   entity claims directly) is preferred on those buildings. ⚠️
4. **Basis reduction.** Subsidized financing (tax-exempt bonds, soft loans, below-market land) can

entries.question: 7. Risk & mitigation (summary)
entries.answer:
reduce **eligible basis**, shrinking the credit. ⚠️
5. **Aggregation (PWA / domestic-content cliff).** A multi-site rollout that aggregates above 1 MW can
   lose the small-facility PWA exemption and convert a domestic-content miss into an all-or-nothing
   loss. ⚠️
6. **Power-contract form (if compute/offtake is involved).** A volumetric ($/kWh) sale can
   recharacterize as a lease and void the structure; a §7701(e) service-contract form is load-bearing. ⚠️

---

entries.section: 8. The validation discipline (why these numbers are trustworthy)
entries.question: 8. The validation discipline (why these numbers are trustworthy)
entries.answer:
This is the differentiator. Every load-bearing claim is tracked in a **validation ledger** and moves
through one lifecycle:

> **v0 (asserted) → grounded (cited to primary law) → confirmed (counsel/CPA sign-off) → sealed
> (provenance stamp).**

A claim is presented to a counterparty **only at the confidence its status earns** — settled-law items
are stated plainly; fact-specific items are flagged as counsel determinations. The legal *mechanism* in
this paper (direct pay, §761(a), the low-income bonus, the clocks) is **grounded in primary law** (IRS
guidance, the Code, the Treasury regulations). The remaining open items are **explicitly the fact-specific
gates** a transaction must clear — they are not hidden. This is the opposite of a pro-forma that asserts
a headline IRR and hopes; it is an auditable chain from claim to authority.

---

entries.question: 9. Illustrative economics (hypothetical — not a projection)
entries.answer:
For a *single illustrative building* with a ~$500k battery, ~$200k of chargers, and a small rooftop
solar array, with **round, hypothetical** inputs:

- **Battery §48E** at a 30–40% rate → ~$150k–$200k of credit. ✅ (rate is law; the dollar base is illustrative)
- **Solar §48E** at 30% **+20% low-income** → up to ~50% of the solar cost. ✅
- **Chargers §30C** at 30% of eligible cost, **only if** placed in service by the deadline at an eligible
  tract — otherwise **$0** (the realistic near-term case). ✅
- **Monetization:** a government-sponsored building takes the above as **100¢ Treasury cash** (Doors 2/3);
  a taxable-owner building takes ~90¢ via transfer (Door 1). The **8–12¢ delta**, across a portfolio, is
  the prize that justifies the §761(a) structuring work. ⚠️ (cents quote-dependent)

The portfolio number is the per-building figure × the count of buildings that clear each gate — which is
exactly what the validation ledger and the deal-specific matrix exist to compute. **No portfolio total is
asserted here.**

---
